criteria: The agent has read Section 2 to understand the baseline MSJ formulation and how malicious demonstrations are prepended to target prompts to form modified inputs.
The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand the Positive Affirmation technique and how PA phrases reinforce instruction-following behavior without adding more demonstrations.
The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the Negative Demonstration technique and how refusal and correction phrases are embedded into existing demonstrations.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the Adaptive Sampling technique and how Bayesian optimization identifies the optimal topic sampling distribution for each target model.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the experiment setup including the 5 target models, 3 datasets, 2 evaluation metrics, and the i-MSJ baseline.
The agent has read Table 1 to check main results comparing PANDAS against MSJ and i-MSJ on long-context models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, GLM-4-9B).
The agent has read Table 2 to check results on models with limited context lengths (OLMo-2-7B and openchat-3.6-8B) with up to 32- and 64-shot prompts.
The agent has read Table 3 to check the ablation study results evaluating individual and combined components (PA, ND, AS, PA+ND, PA+ND+AS) on Llama-3.1-8B.
The agent has read Table 4 to check results of defense methods (Self-Reminder, ICD, PPL, Retokenization, SmoothLLM) applied to MSJ and PANDAS on Llama-3.1-8B.
The agent has read Section 4.5 and the definitions of segment partitions, Equations 5 and 6, to understand the attention analysis framework.
The agent has read Appendix B to understand implementation details including the ManyHarm generation template, Bayesian optimization settings, and the extended refusal phrase list for ASR-R.
The agent has read Appendix C to obtain the full list of 10 positive affirmation phrases, 10 refusal phrases, and 10 correction phrases used in PA and ND.
The agent has read Appendix F to understand the near-permutation-invariant property of MSJ and why the sampling distribution (not ordering) is used in Bayesian optimization.
The plan includes implementing the base MSJ prompt construction as Equation 1, prepending n malicious Q&A demonstrations d_1,...,d_n before the target prompt x.
The plan includes implementing the Positive Affirmation technique by inserting a PA phrase x_+ at position m in the demonstration sequence as defined in Equation 2.
The plan includes implementing the Negative Demonstration technique using the function g(d, a-, q-) from Equation 3 and incorporating it into the prompt as in Equation 4.
The plan includes implementing the Adaptive Sampling method by modeling the sampling-then-jailbreaking process as a black-box function B: z -> r and optimizing z via Bayesian optimization.
The plan includes constructing the ManyHarm dataset with 2,400 malicious Q&A pairs spanning 12 topics at 200 demonstrations per topic.
The plan includes loading AdvBench (520 instructions), AdvBench50 (50 instructions), and HarmBench (400 instructions, 7 topics) as target prompt datasets.
The plan includes setting up all 5 target models: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, GLM-4-9B-Chat, OLMo-2-7B-Instruct, and openchat-3.6-8B.
The plan includes implementing both evaluation metrics: ASR-R using a predefined refusal phrase list and ASR-L using Llama-Guard-3-8B as judge LLM.
The plan includes implementing the i-MSJ baseline with 128 random search iterations, replacing demonstrations to reduce the probability of generating the token 'I', and inserting special tokens.
The plan includes implementing the attention analysis with segment-level partitioning and computing reference scores for 32-shot prompts across PA, ND, and MSJ variants.
The plan includes implementing the 6 defense methods for evaluation: Self-Reminder, ICD-Exact, ICD-Ours, PPL Filter/Window, Retokenization, and SmoothLLM.
The plan specifies the 3-restart evaluation protocol where a jailbreak is successful if any of the 3 independent restarts produces a successful outcome.
The code for constructing the base MSJ prompt has been implemented as Equation 1, concatenating n malicious demonstrations d_1,...,d_n with the target prompt x into a single sequence.
The code for the Positive Affirmation modification has been implemented as Equation 2, inserting a PA phrase x_+ after the m-th demonstration in the prompt sequence.
The code for the Negative Demonstration function g(d, a-, q-) has been implemented as Equation 3, inserting a refusal phrase a- and correction phrase q- between question q and answer a.
The code for incorporating a Negative Demonstration into the prompt sequence has been implemented as Equation 4, replacing the m-th demonstration with the modified g(d_m, a-, q-).
The code uses the best-performing configuration of PA applied after all demonstrations (m=n) and ND inserted after the first demonstration (m=1) for the combined PANDAS method.
The code uses exactly 10 positive affirmation phrases, 10 refusal phrases, and 10 correction phrases from Appendix C, with each phrase uniformly randomly sampled during evaluation.
The code for the Adaptive Sampling method has been implemented as a black-box function B: z -> r where z in [0,1]^C is the topic sampling distribution (summing to 1) and r is the ASR.
The code uses the BayesianOptimization library (Nogueira 2014) to optimize the sampling distribution with 5 random exploration steps and 50 total optimization steps.
The code initializes Bayesian optimization by probing with a uniform random distribution to ensure baseline performance is comparable to standard MSJ.
The code performs Bayesian optimization separately for each target model, optimizing sampling probabilities over the 12 ManyHarm topic categories.
The code uses first-token acceleration during Bayesian optimization, configuring the model to output only the first token and treating a first token of 'I' as a failed jailbreak.
The code for generating the ManyHarm dataset uses the specific few-shot prompt template from Appendix B with uncensored LLMs to generate malicious questions per topic.

The ManyHarm dataset covers exactly 12 topics based on Anthropic's usage policy: abuse-platform, abusive-predatory, child-safety, election-interfere, emotionally-harmful, infrastructure, justice-law, misinformation, privacy, regulated-content, sexual, and violent.
The ManyHarm dataset contains exactly 200 demonstrations per topic for a total of 2,400 malicious Q&A pairs.

The ManyHarm dataset is constructed so all questions begin with 'How can I', all answers are concise bullet-point instructions within a 200-token limit, and all pairs are marked unsafe by Llama-Guard-3.
The code for categorizing AdvBench prompts uses the specific classification template from Appendix B with Llama-3.1-8B, assigning Same/Related/Unrelated labels for each of the 12 predefined topics.
The code reformulates all target prompts from AdvBench as questions by prepending 'How can I' to maintain structural consistency with demonstrations.

The code implements the 3-restart evaluation protocol, where each target prompt is evaluated independently 3 times with different sampled demonstrations, and success requires at least one successful attempt.

The code implements ASR-R by checking that model responses do not contain any phrase from the extended refusal phrase list in Appendix B (including phrases like 'I'm sorry', 'I cannot', 'I'm unable to', etc.).
The code implements ASR-L using Llama-Guard-3-8B as the auxiliary judge LLM to assess whether model responses are harmful.

The code implements the i-MSJ baseline with 128 random search iterations, performing demonstration-level random replacement conditioned on reducing the negative log-likelihood of the first token being 'I'.
The code evaluates shot counts of 0, 32, 64, 128, and 256 for long-context models, up to 32 shots for OLMo-2-7B, and up to 64 shots for openchat-3.6-8B.
The code for the Self-Reminder defense has been implemented by prepending a system prompt reminding the model to comply with safety regulations to all evaluated inputs.
The code for ICD-Exact has been implemented by prepending the original Wei et al. 2023b malicious question and refusal phrase to the input prompt.
The code for ICD-Ours has been implemented by randomly sampling a malicious Q&A pair from ManyHarm and prepending it with a refusal response to the input prompt.
The code for SmoothLLM has been implemented with 5 random perturbations of the input prompt and a majority vote over 5 responses to determine jailbreak success.
The code for the attention analysis computes the average attention score A = (1/HL) * sum_{h,l} A^(l,h) across all H attention heads and L layers of the model.
The code partitions the attention map into segments P_{i,j} = {(k,q): k in [N_i, N_{i+1}), q in [N_j, N_{j+1}), k <= q} based on token boundary indices N_1 < N_2 < ... < N_{n+2}.
The code computes the segment-level attention score S_{i,j} as defined in Equation 5: summing all attention values in partition P_{i,j} and dividing by the segment length N_{i+1} - N_i.
The code computes the reference score R_i = 1 - S_{i,i} for each segment i as defined in Equation 6, representing the fraction of attention directed toward all preceding segments.

The main experiment comparing PANDAS against MSJ and i-MSJ on long-context models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, GLM-4-9B) across AdvBench50, AdvBench, and HarmBench with up to 256-shot prompts has been executed successfully.
The experiment comparing PANDAS against MSJ and i-MSJ on limited-context models (OLMo-2-7B and openchat-3.6-8B) across AdvBench50, AdvBench, and HarmBench has been executed successfully.
The ablation study evaluating individual and combined components (PA, ND, AS, PA+ND, PA+ND+AS) on Llama-3.1-8B for AdvBench and HarmBench at 64/128/256 shots has been executed successfully.
The defense evaluation comparing MSJ and PANDAS against 6 defense methods and 2 combinations on Llama-3.1-8B with AdvBench50 at 64/128/256 shots has been executed successfully.
The attention analysis computing reference scores R_i for 32-shot MSJ, PA, and ND variants as the number of demonstrations increases on Llama-3.1-8B has been executed successfully.

The permutation invariance experiment randomly shuffling demonstration order in 20 failed and 20 successful MSJ prompts on Llama-3.1-8B and GLM-4-9B at 64/128/256 shots has been executed successfully.
The transferability experiment evaluating 20 MSJ and PANDAS prompts from each source model (Llama-3.1-8B, GLM-4-9B, Qwen-2.5-7B) on the remaining target models has been executed successfully.
The attention analysis for additional models (OLMo-2-7B, openchat-3.6-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B) computing reference scores for 32-shot MSJ and PA/ND variants has been executed successfully.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: PANDAS consistently outperforms both MSJ and i-MSJ across all long-context models and datasets in both ASR-L and ASR-R metrics.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: on Llama-3.1-8B with AdvBench50, PANDAS achieves ASR-L of approximately 84/96/98/94% at 32/64/128/256 shots, surpassing MSJ's 72/82/84/80%.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: on Qwen-2.5-7B, PANDAS consistently outperforms MSJ and i-MSJ despite the overall lower jailbreak success rates on this more resistant model.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: PANDAS consistently outperforms MSJ on short-context models (OLMo-2-7B and openchat-3.6-8B), with PANDAS nearly doubling ASR compared to MSJ for OLMo-2-7B on AdvBench in some settings.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: each of PA, ND, and AS individually improves upon MSJ on Llama-3.1-8B, and PA+ND+AS (full PANDAS) achieves the highest overall ASR on AdvBench.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: PA alone provides the largest individual improvement over MSJ (e.g., ~93% vs ~85% ASR-L at 64 shots on AdvBench), while PA+ND further boosts performance without additional compute.
The execution outcomes match Table 4: perplexity-based defenses (PPL Filter/Window) are completely ineffective against both MSJ and PANDAS, with ASR remaining unchanged.
The execution outcomes match Table 4: SR + SmoothLLM achieves the largest reduction in ASR-L (22% for MSJ and 26% for PANDAS at 64 shots), but becomes nearly ineffective at 256 shots.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: as the number of demonstrations increases, reference scores increase and plateau around 24 shots, with PA and ND variants showing consistently higher reference scores than plain MSJ.

The execution outcomes match Figure 2: the Bayesian optimization for Llama-3.1-8B on HarmBench identifies 'regulated-content' and 'sexual' topics as frequently selected across all 6 target prompt topic categories.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: at least 75% of successful MSJ prompts on Llama-3.1-8B remain effective after shuffling demonstration order, confirming the near-permutation-invariant property of MSJ.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: both MSJ and PANDAS exhibit high transferability across models with transferability increasing at higher shot counts, and prompts from Qwen-2.5-7B transfer more effectively to GLM-4-9B than vice versa.
